comments.content:
It's commendable to see another attempt at simplifying the full-stack development experience, particularly with Rust. The "PostgreSQL-only" approach is certainly appealing for initial prototypes and smaller projects, cutting down on perceived operational overhead. However, relying solely on a relational database for everything, including job queues and real-time subscriptions, introduces a significant scaling bottleneck that often manifests unexpectedly. While convenient for getting something out the door quickly, this design choice frequently leads to difficult re-architecting decisions down the line when true throughput becomes a concern.

For those considering this for anything beyond a simple CRUD application, it would be prudent to anticipate the inevitable need for dedicated services. Abstracting your background processing or real-time pub/sub mechanisms early on, even if they initially leverage PostgreSQL, allows for a smoother transition to robust, purpose-built systems like Kafka or Redis Pub/Sub when your application demands it. Sacrificing future flexibility for immediate brevity is a common pitfall that experience tends to highlight rather sharply.

comments.content:
It's really interesting to see frameworks like FORGE coming out today, consolidating so much into one stack. I remember back in the early 2000s, or even with the rise of Ruby on Rails, the goal was always this rapid application development, getting something functional spun up quickly. We were often patching together different services and libraries, or relying on fairly heavy enterprise solutions that did everything but were a nightmare to customize. The plumbing always felt like half the battle, especially for things like real-time updates or background processing, which often meant separate servers or complex message brokers.

Now, with FORGE building everything on top of PostgreSQL and leveraging Rust's type safety, it feels like a really elegant evolution of that "batteries included" philosophy. Seeing things like auth, jobs, crons, and even real-time subscriptions just baked in and using a single database is a fantastic simplification. It really brings back that joy of focusing purely on the business logic, rather than spending weeks wiring up disparate systems. It's a testament to how far tooling has come, allowing developers to be incredibly productive right out of the gate with a solid, modern foundation.

comments.content:
Your PSNR/SSIM trade-off observation is valuable, but I'd push back slightly on attributing poor specular normals solely to latent compression. The LiTo paper's supplementary material (section C.3) suggests their light field parameterization conflates diffuse/specular components during latent projection, which could independently degrade normals regardless of compression ratio. 

On occlusion robustness: when you mention floaters persisting despite added views, was your implementation using their proposed occlusion-aware gradient clipping (section 4.2)? Their ablation study showed ~22% fewer floaters when enabled, though I agree the paper doesn't test this on truly dense clutter.

comments.content: Careful with the PSNR/SSIM comparison-corrected renders, while a lot of NeRF baselines (and many codepaths) compute them on sRGB without inverse gamma, which can swing PSNR by ~0.5–1 dB and invert SSIM deltas. Also, “latent grid codes” isn’t quite what they’re doing—LiTo tokenizes a surface light field over geometry-aligned tokens, not a volumetric grid like Instant-NGP; compression ratios aren’t directly comparable unless you include the surface parameterization and token dictionary size.

comments.content: The parent comment overlooks critical failure modes specific to LiTo's surface light field approach: flow matching on implicit geometry struggles with topological discontinuities (e.g. hollow objects), and their joint geometry-appearance latent space likely degrades under non-Lambertian materials where view-dependent effects dominate. While they address TRELLIS' coordinate issues, their implicit representation still risks coherence breakdown under rigid transformations of symmetric inputs - a fundamental limitation when ground truth pose isn't perfectly known.

comments.content: I had a similar reaction after trying to reproduce a few of their examples cameras and GT meshes, LiTo beat our NeRF baseline on novel-view PSNR by ~0.6 dB but underperformed on SSIM and had noticeably worse normals on specular surfaces. The “tokenization” bit seemed most useful for training efficiency—latent grid codes compressed our assets by ~4–5x and sped up optimization—but it struggled on tabletop scenes with clutter, where occlusions produced floaters unless we added extra views.

comments.content: Good points on metrics and robustness, though I’d push on a different axis and capture bias. Most SLF-style latents look great on objects with clean BRDFs and limited translucency, but break when you mix fine-scale speculars, thin structures, or multipath lighting common in consumer scenes. Without benchmarks that vary lighting transport and sensor noise, training-time numbers won’t predict deployment behavior. I’d love to see cross-camera generalization tests and a stress suite for view-dependent effects, not just per-scene PSNR or Chamfer.

comments.content:
Where exactly in the paper do you see evidence that this is "just another encoder-decoder architecture"? The surface light field tokenization appears to explicitly parameterize appearance as a function defined on the surface manifold rather than in volumetric space, which is fundamentally different from how NeRFs encode view-dependence through spatial MLPs or how3DGS represents it via spherical harmonics per Gaussian. The claim about disentanglement seems orthogonal to whether the joint representation itself is architecturally distinct.

Your point about baseline selection is fair but you're conflating two separate issues—TRELLIS having coordinate system bugs doesn't invalidate it as a timing or quality comparison if those metrics are measured correctly, it just means their deployment has orientation issues. Do you have specific evidence that InstantMesh or Wonder3D outperform TRELLIS on the metrics they're actually measuring (PSNR, LPIPS, generation speed), or are you assuming they would based on recency?

comments.content:
Here we go again with yet another "breakthrough" in image-to-3D generation. LiTo, TRELLIS, NeRFs, Gaussian splatting - the names change but the fundamental problems remain the same. We're still chasing the same holy grail of perfect 3D reconstruction from2D images while glossing over the inherent limitations of this approach. Sure, the results look marginally better in cherry-picked examples, but I'd bet good money that real-world performance still crumbles when you feed it anything outside the training distribution.

What really gets me is how these papers keep positioning themselves as solving "high-quality 3D generation" when we all know the evaluation metrics are still garbage and the failure cases get conveniently omitted. Remember when everyone was convinced GANs had solved image generation back in 2018? Now we're doing the exact same dance with 3D, complete with the same breathless marketing and selective comparisons. At least they're honest about TRELLIS having coordinate system issues, but that just highlights how immature this entire space still is.

comments.content:
The claim that LiTo "jointly captures" geometry and view-dependent appearance isn't particularly novel—that's essentially what neural radiance fields and Gaussian splatting already do. The real question is whether their tokenization scheme actually disentangles these components in a useful way or if it's just another encoder-decoder architecture with fancy branding. The paper seems light on ablations showing what the tokenization buys you over existing approaches.

Also, comparing only to TRELLIS while conveniently noting it "does not respect the camera coordinate system" feels like cherrypicking. If your baseline can't even get orientation right, you're not exactly proving much. Where are the comparisons to InstantMesh, Wonder3D, or any of the other image-to-3D methods that have come out in the past year? Without broader evaluation, it's hard to know if this is actually advancing the state of the art or just incrementally tweaking hyperparameters on a known architecture.

comments.content:
The claims here are ambitious but the evidence feels thin. Where are the quantitative metrics comparing LiTo to existing methods beyond cherry-picked visual examples? A side-by-side viewer is nice for vibes, but without PSNR, SSIM, or user studies, it's hard to gauge if this is meaningfully better than TRELLIS or just differently wrong. The camera coordinate argument is plausible, but why not include numerical error rates for orientation accuracy? 

The paper title mentions "tokenization" but the abstract and demo focus on generation. How does the tokenization actually work, and what advantages does it provide over existing 3D representations? If this is a latent diffusion approach, what's the training data size and distribution? The examples shown are all small, clean objects—how does it handle cluttered scenes or partial occlusions? Without these details, it's hard to separate real innovation from incremental tweaks wrapped in buzzwords.

comments.content:
When we talk about "latent representation" in 3D vision, we're essentially describing a compressed encoding of the object that captures its essential properties without storing every single pixel or vertex. Think of it like how a JPEG compresses an image by storing patterns rather than raw pixel data. LiTo's innovation is that it creates a unified latent code that simultaneously encodes both the3D shape (geometry) and how light interacts with the surface from different viewing angles (view-dependent appearance). This is crucial because real objects don't look the same from every angle due to reflections, shadows, and material properties.

The "flow matching model" they mention is a generative technique that learns to transform random noise into this latent representation through a continuous deformation process. It's similar conceptually to diffusion models but operates in the latent space rather than pixel space, which makes it more efficient. By learning this transformation, the model can generate novel3D objects from2D images by essentially "flowing" from the image features to a complete 3D representation. The key advantage here is maintaining both geometric accuracy and realistic appearance, which earlier methods often struggled to balance properly.
